query: Performance Requirements for Portable Fire Extinguishers

outs.instruction: I’m trying to pin down how long a portable fire extinguisher is expected to keep working once it’s activated, because that timing is what I need to compare across products. Please focus on tables that connect an extinguisher’s extinguishing rating (like A- or B-class ratings) to a required minimum effective discharge time in seconds. I want the result to stay centered on that discharge-time requirement rather than drifting into other performance topics. Tables that mainly talk about fill quantities, fill tolerances, agent mass, or minimum rating thresholds without specifying any minimum discharge time aren’t what I’m looking for. Even if they are clearly about extinguisher performance, they don’t help me answer the specific timing question.

For this, I only care about the minimum effective discharge time that a portable fire extinguisher must achieve for a given extinguishing rating, because I’m checking whether devices meet the timing requirement. I need tables that are purely about the mapping between rating and minimum effective discharge time (in seconds), without mixing in any other performance requirements. When discharge time is bundled together with agent fill mass, extinguisher media types, minimum rating thresholds, or manufacturing tolerances, it becomes hard to isolate what I’m verifying. So please exclude tables that focus on quantities or rating thresholds and only incidentally relate to timing, or that combine timing with other specification dimensions. I’m looking for a clean, timing-only view of the requirement.

I’m trying to check whether different portable extinguishers meet the practical performance thresholds they’re supposed to achieve in real use. The most useful tables for me are ones where at least one full column is about the required performance level itself, such as the fire rating level the unit must achieve, so I can compare the target ratings across options. It also helps if another column is about an operational performance requirement during use, like how long it must effectively discharge, because that is part of the performance requirement too. I don’t want tables where the columns mainly describe manufacturing or filling allowances, like how much deviation in fill quantity is permitted, since that’s more of a production tolerance than a performance requirement. If a table devotes a column primarily to those tolerance/allowance details, I’d rather exclude it even if it still mentions extinguisher types.

I’m trying to pin down what these portable extinguisher performance requirements actually mean in practice, so I can quickly check whether a unit meets the standard. The most useful tables for me are the ones that tie the required performance to specific charge sizes of the extinguisher (like kilograms for powder/CO₂/clean agent or liters for water-based), because that’s what I can verify from a product label. If the performance is only shown as a rating code without showing the corresponding fill quantity, I can’t map it to the extinguisher I’m looking at. So please focus on tables where the extinguisher is broken down by fill quantity and paired with the required extinguishing rating. Tables that talk only about discharge time or tolerance rules are still in the same topic area, but they don’t help me match a rating requirement to a particular sized extinguisher.

I’m also not looking for thickness-only tolerance tables even if they use similar precision labels, because that’s a separate acceptance criterion. The most helpful tables are the ones where some columns consistently describe allowed dimensional deviation amounts (like plus/minus limits or deviation ranges) that I can compare across different size bands. It’s also important that those deviation-focused columns are organized by the product’s size categories (for example, grouped by thickness, width, or length ranges), so I can match a measured coil or sheet to the right tolerance. I’m not looking for tables where the emphasis shifts to production or coating process details, chemical composition, or mechanical test results, because those columns don’t help me judge dimensional acceptance. If a table includes columns mainly describing process settings or material properties rather than dimensional deviation limits, I want to leave it out even if it’s still about hot-dip coated steel.

I’m looking up the allowable size variations for this hot-dip coated steel product so I can check whether supplied material is within spec. What matters most to me is how the tolerance changes depending on which accuracy class the sheet/strip is ordered to. So the table needs to split the tolerances into the two precision options (the regular grade versus the higher-accuracy grade) so I can compare them side by side for the same nominal size. Tables that only give a single tolerance value without clearly separating the two precision levels aren’t as useful for my purpose, even if they’re still about dimensional tolerances. query: Mobile Augmented Reality System Enumeration Data Types

outs.instruction: I’m trying to wire up an AR session and I mainly need the enums that control how the system behaves at runtime, not the ones that describe sensor output formats. The useful tables for me are the ones about session configuration and tracking behavior, like how tracking is started, what kinds of planes can be detected, what camera direction is selected, or what query mode is used for intersecting with detected geometry. Tables that are about media/image formats, light estimation status, or recognition categories feel more like output metadata than knobs I can configure. Those should be excluded since they don’t help me decide how to initialize or steer the AR session.

I’m currently focused on plane detection behavior, because I’m debugging why surfaces are or aren’t being found in my AR scene. If a table mixes plane detection with other session controls like camera facing direction, tracking run mode, or intersection query modes, it becomes harder for me to isolate just the plane-related options I’m working on right now. Likewise, tables about pixel formats, illumination estimation state, hand gestures, or face augmentation aren’t relevant to this specific issue. Please only include results that stay centered on plane detection modes without branching into other AR features.

I’m trying to wire up an AR mobile SDK and I keep running into those fixed option sets where a single integer maps to a specific runtime behavior. I don’t want tables where the columns are mainly about feature category write-ups or marketing-style descriptions, with the constants appearing only as a secondary reference. I’m trying to wire up an AR session and keep mixing up which constants control the runtime behavior versus everything else. I’m trying to look up the constants used by a mobile AR SDK so I can set the right modes and interpret what the API returns. query: List of unit process activity data for chemical fiber production

outs.instruction: I’m trying to understand what kinds of supporting operations sit around chemical fiber manufacturing and what activity data they typically track. Please focus on tables that explicitly describe the auxiliary production system or ancillary production system, especially where they list utilities like steam, nitrogen, compressed air, electricity, and water, plus the waste streams that need disposal. What I need is the kind of “plant support” inputs and outputs that are shared across a production line, not just the chemistry of making the fiber itself. Tables that only talk about the main production system for a specific fiber and stop there don’t help me compare these supporting processes. So exclude results that are limited to the core conversion steps without the auxiliary/ancillary system context.

I’m narrowing this down to the activity data for the supporting utility and waste-handling side of chemical fiber production, because that’s what I’m benchmarking across plants. I only want tables that are centered on the auxiliary production system and/or ancillary production system, where the content is basically about utilities (steam, compressed air, nitrogen, fuel, electricity, water) and the outputs like wastewater, waste yarn, hazardous waste, or CO2. If a table mixes those support-system items together with a detailed main production system recipe for the fiber line, it becomes hard for me to isolate the utilities and waste part. In other words, I’m looking for results where the support system is the sole focus rather than just one section alongside the main process. Please leave out tables that primarily describe the main production system or combine both main and auxiliary systems in the same table.

I’m trying to map out what information is typically tracked for the different steps involved in making chemical fibers, so I can compare the production stages in a consistent way. The most helpful tables are the ones where at least one column is devoted to describing, in full sentences or long phrases, the kinds of inputs and outputs involved in each step (for example, what goes in, what comes out, and what waste streams are generated). I also want tables where the remaining descriptive column is about the production stage or system being discussed, so it’s clear which part of the overall lifecycle the activity data belongs to. What I don’t need are tables where the detailed columns are mainly about resources, energy use, or pollution figures as the primary focus, because that shifts away from the process-level input/output inventory I’m trying to list. If a table’s key descriptive column is centered on emissions/consumption categories rather than describing the material and utility flows for each unit process, it shouldn’t be included. Basically, keep it focused on process descriptions plus their input/output activity data, not on environmental indicator-style summaries.

I’m pulling together a clear reference of what gets consumed and what comes out during the manufacturing of different chemical fibers. The most useful tables are the ones where each row is centered on a specific unit process stage, and the next column spells out the corresponding activity data for that stage. I want the unit-process rows to explicitly break the production into parts like raw/auxiliary materials acquisition versus the main production system and the auxiliary/ancillary system, so I can compare stages consistently. Please focus on tables that describe the activity data in terms of inputs/outputs (materials, utilities like electricity/steam/water, and wastes/emissions) for each unit process. Tables that instead list fiber products as the main rows, or tables that track a single fiber across different dates/locations without listing unit processes as the row items, should be excluded because they don’t let me map activity data to each process step.

I’m trying to pull together activity data for the different steps involved in making these fibers, mainly so I can see what gets consumed and what comes out at each stage. What’s most useful to me is when the table breaks the process into separate parts like the main production system versus the auxiliary/ancillary systems, since those have very different kinds of inputs and wastes. So I want tables where the unit process is shown in that split way, with a distinct row for the main production system and another for the auxiliary/ancillary side. If a table only gives one combined production-stage row without separating those systems, it’s harder for me to compare across fibers. Those single-row summaries are still on-topic, but they aren’t detailed in the way I need for organizing the dataset.

query: Starter performance test requirements

outs.instruction: I’m trying to pin down the test requirements that verify a starter can repeatedly make and break a load under specified operating conditions. The tables I want should spell out the on/off test conditions in a structured way, like the utilization category (e.g., AC-3), the current and voltage ratios, the power factor, and the required energizing/de-energizing times, ideally with the required number of operating cycles. This kind of content helps me understand the endurance and switching-duty expectations, not just a single stress event. If a table mainly focuses on short-circuit breaking capability (kA-level prospective or ultimate breaking current) or only lists a breaking-current test count/interval without the broader switching-duty conditions, it’s not what I’m after. Please exclude those short-circuit-centric test requirement tables because they don’t reflect the repeated operational duty I’m checking.

For this topic, I only need the requirements that describe short-circuit breaking performance testing for starters/contactor-based starters. I’m looking for tables that are centered on kA-level prospective current or ultimate breaking current, along with any associated test setup factors like U/Ue, cosφ, energizing time, interval time, or number of tests. It’s important that the table stays focused on that short-circuit test requirement rather than mixing it with general making/breaking duty cycles, utilization-category switching conditions, or overload withstand currents. Those mixed tables make it hard for me to isolate the short-circuit test requirements I’m documenting. So please leave out any table that primarily describes operational switching endurance (cycles, I/Ie duty ratios) or overload withstand tests instead of the short-circuit breaking test itself.

I’m putting together a checklist for verifying whether a starter can pass the required performance tests, so I need tables that spell out the actual test conditions rather than general descriptions. The most useful tables are the ones where some columns consistently describe how the device is switched during the test, like timing for energizing and de‑energizing and how many operations or cycles are required. I also want the conditions to be comparable across current ranges, so it helps if the table includes columns that define the electrical stress applied during making/breaking (for example the current/voltage factors or power factor used). What I don’t need are tables that mostly just map a rated current range to a single prospective/ultimate current value without any timing or cycling context, because that doesn’t tell me how to run the test. Similarly, tables that focus mainly on one isolated timing value with no broader test-condition columns are not a good fit for the checklist I’m building.

I’m trying to set up the acceptance/performance testing for the starter, so I need the requirements laid out in a way I can apply to different sizes of devices. The most helpful tables are the ones where each row represents a specific starter utilization category and rated operational current range, and the columns give the required test conditions for that exact case. I especially want tables that spell out the making and breaking requirements together, including the current/voltage ratios, power factor, energizing/de-energizing times, and how many operating cycles to run. If a table only lists short-circuit/ultimate breaking test currents or prospective currents without the full on/off duty details, it won’t work for what I’m doing. Also, tables that only talk about a single timing parameter (like just de-energizing time versus a test current) are too incomplete for my purpose.

I’m trying to pin down the practical test setup details for this starter-related performance testing, not just a list of current categories. The most useful tables for me are the ones that spell out the timing of the test actions so I can understand how long the device is held on and how long it is held off during each test. So I want tables where the rows are still organized around the same rated operational current ranges, but the test requirements are expressed with explicit on/off timing values (energizing time and de-energizing time, or an equivalent interval time). Tables that only give current ratios, power factor, or prospective/ultimate breaking currents without any on/off timing are still about performance testing, but they don’t tell me how the test is actually performed. Please prioritize results presented with those time fields over tables that focus purely on electrical magnitude conditions.

query: Energy Efficiency Indicators for Refrigeration and Air-Conditioning Equipment

outs.instruction: I’m trying to compare air-conditioning and refrigeration equipment in a way that reflects how efficient they are over typical real-world operation, not just at one test point. Tables are only useful to me if they explicitly include APF values (or APF thresholds) for the equipment types, since that’s the indicator I’m using to line up products across categories. I’m fine if those APF entries are grouped by unit type or capacity range, as long as APF is clearly present as an efficiency indicator. What I don’t want are tables that only provide other metrics like SEER, IPLV, COP, HSPF, or EER without any APF information, because then I can’t make the comparisons I need. So even if a table is about efficiency, if APF isn’t part of it, it should be left out.

For this topic, I’m focusing specifically on APF as the efficiency yardstick, because I’m trying to screen equipment based on seasonal/annual performance rather than a mix of different rating systems. The most helpful results are tables that are essentially centered on APF itself—showing APF benchmark/product values or APF thresholds by unit type and capacity—without also bundling in other efficiency indicators. When a table mixes APF together with SEER, IPLV, EER, COP, or HSPF, it becomes harder for me to isolate the one metric I actually need. I’m not looking for a general efficiency “dashboard”; I want a clean APF-only view that I can quickly use for comparison. Anything that treats APF as just one column among several other metrics should be excluded for my purpose.

I’m trying to compare how efficient these HVAC and refrigeration units are in actual operation, using the standardized efficiency indicators rather than marketing descriptions. The most useful tables for me are the ones where several columns are clearly devoted to efficiency metrics that reflect performance in cooling or heating mode, so I can scan across different unit categories and capacities. It also really helps if the table distinguishes efficiency targets at different levels (for example, baseline versus a higher-tier value) as part of those performance-focused columns. I’m not trying to look up regulatory document references or where the thresholds come from; that’s not what I’m comparing right now. So I want to avoid tables where any major columns are mainly occupied by citations to standards, index-class wording, or compliance text, because that pushes the table away from being a direct efficiency-indicator comparison.

I’m trying to quickly compare energy-efficiency indicators across different kinds of refrigeration and air-conditioning equipment, mainly to see what each category is expected to meet. The most useful table for me is one where each row is a specific equipment type (for example, air-cooled cooling-only units or floor radiant type systems), and the columns list the efficiency indicators for that type. Please prioritize tables that explicitly show the indicator names like SEER, APF, IPLV, HSPF, EER, or COP/ACOP alongside “green benchmark product value” and “green product value”, so it’s clear how the thresholds are presented. I don’t want tables that are organized mainly by capacity ranges (kW bands) with the equipment type only playing a secondary role, because that makes it hard to scan by equipment category. Also exclude tables that mostly cite external standard class references (like “Class 1/2 index specified in GB…”) without actually presenting comparable indicator fields for each equipment type in the table itself.

I’m trying to look up energy-efficiency indicators for the kinds of cooling and air-conditioning units covered here, mainly so I can compare the efficiency requirements across different kinds of equipment. What matters to me is seeing the efficiency metrics organized around cooling-capacity ranges, because I need to tell how the indicator thresholds change when the unit size changes. So I want tables where each equipment type is broken into specific rated cooling capacity bands (for example, ≤14 kW, 7–14 kW, >14 kW, and similar ranges) and then the efficiency indicators are listed for those bands. Tables that talk about the same topic but switch to heating-capacity bands instead are not what I’m looking for, because they don’t let me compare the cooling-side sizing cutoffs. I’d also skip tables that don’t actually lay out any capacity ranges as separate entries, since they don’t help me see how the requirements vary by size.

query: Classification of Additive Manufacturing Process Raw Materials and Activation Sources

outs.instruction: I’m trying to narrow this topic down to cases where the energy input is clearly based on a laser, because I’m comparing laser-driven approaches across different feedstocks. What I need are tables that explicitly connect raw material categories to specific laser-based AM process names, so I can see which material types pair with which laser routes. If a table mainly focuses on activation sources that are not lasers (like chemical energy or a grab-bag of heat sources) and laser is missing or not central, it won’t help me. I also want to avoid tables that are essentially about non-laser families and only happen to mention lasers in passing, because that muddies the comparison I’m trying to make. So only keep tables where laser activation is a clear, meaningful part of the classification itself.

Within this classification topic, I only want tables that are purely about laser-activated AM routes and how they map to different raw material types. I’m using the results to build a clean reference list of laser-based categories, so I don’t want the laser entries mixed together with electron-beam, arc, plasma, chemical-energy, or other activation sources in the same table. Tables that present multiple activation sources side by side make it harder for me to isolate the laser-only mapping without extra filtering. Even if those tables include laser columns, they’re not what I’m looking for because the focus is split across several energy sources. Please return only tables where the classification stays centered on laser activation throughout.

I’m trying to quickly map which kinds of feedstocks go with which ways of supplying energy in additive manufacturing, so I can compare the same material family across different energy inputs. The tables that help most are the ones where some columns are dedicated to energy-input categories (like beam-based or heat/chemical-based activation) and the entries under those columns are specific AM process routes tied to that energy input. I also want the material side to stay at a broad family level (e.g., metals, polymers, inorganic non-metals, biomaterials) rather than drifting into very detailed formulations. Please avoid tables where any column mainly describes a particular deposition form or feedstock physical format (such as focusing on “wire vs powder vs resin” as the main organizing idea), because that makes it harder for me to compare activation sources cleanly. In other words, I want the columns to be about activation/energy types, not columns that primarily categorize the physical form of the raw material.

I’m trying to quickly map out which additive manufacturing options exist for different feedstocks, so I can compare them in a consistent way. The most useful table for me is one where each row is a single raw material category, and the columns show what kind of energy or activation method is used to process it. I want the cells to name the corresponding AM process names under each activation source so I can see the pairing clearly. Please focus on tables that cover multiple activation sources side by side (for example laser vs electron beam vs arc), not ones that only have one activation source and mostly empty space. Also, I don’t want tables that switch the focus to a specific AM process family as the “main item” and then list materials under it, because I’m trying to start from the material type first.

I’m trying to map out how different feedstocks used in additive manufacturing pair with the energy or activation method that actually drives the process. What would help me most is a table where the material category is the main thing being listed, and the activation sources are broken out as distinct columns so I can scan across and compare them side by side. I specifically want activation sources shown as separate types like laser, electron beam, plasma/arc, chemical energy, or other sources, rather than being buried inside long process-name phrases. If the activation approach is only implied inside a combined process description and there aren’t clear activation-source columns to compare, it’s harder for me to use. So tables that explicitly separate activation sources into their own columns for the same material types are the ones I want to focus on.

query: Quality indices for grey cotton/polyester blended yarn

outs.instruction: I’m trying to screen yarn quality based on how “hairy” the grey cotton/polyester blend is, because that’s what tends to show up later in processing and fabric appearance. Please bring back tables that clearly state hairiness limits and related indicators, like an H value threshold and/or a 2 mm hairiness count, tied to grades or nominal linear density ranges. It’s fine if the table also includes other general quality indices (strength, evenness, neps, defects), as long as hairiness is explicitly part of the specification. What I don’t want are tables that only talk about strength, linear density deviation, evenness, neps, or defects but don’t give any hairiness measures. Those are still quality-related, but they don’t help me compare hairiness performance across the blends.

I’m trying to screen grey cotton/polyester blended yarns based on how “clean and uniform” they are during production, not just whether they are strong enough. The most useful tables for me are the ones that include some columns that describe yarn appearance/regularity issues across grades, like how often thick or thin places show up, how many neps are present, or how even the yarn looks overall. I still want the basic spec context (like the yarn size range and the grading level) so I can compare like with like. What I don’t want is a table where the extra columns are mainly about twist behavior or twist stability, because that shifts the focus away from the surface/irregularity quality I’m checking. If twist-focused descriptions take up a meaningful column in the table, please leave those results out even if they are otherwise about the same blended yarn.

I’m putting together a quick reference for grey cotton/polyester blended yarn so I can compare different yarn specs without digging through a whole standard. The most useful results are tables where each row clearly corresponds to one yarn nominal linear density (in tex), and the other columns list its quality requirements as attributes. Please focus on tables that include the main quality indices together in the same row, especially items like linear density deviation, variation, breaking strength, evenness, neps/defects, and hairiness. I only want tables that explicitly separate the breaking strength requirement by polyester content ranges (for example, one column for 30%≤polyester<50% and another for polyester<30%), because that distinction matters for my use. Tables that don’t show those polyester-range strength columns, or that mainly present twist-only style metrics without the broader yarn quality indices, aren’t what I’m looking for.

I’m trying to compare the quality requirements for this grey cotton/polyester blended yarn in a way that matches how it’s actually specified in standards. What matters most to me is seeing each nominal linear density (tex) paired with the acceptance limits for the key quality indices, so I can line up different rows and grades side by side. For the strength requirement, I specifically need tables that split the breaking strength into two separate columns depending on the polyester content range (one column for 30%≤polyester<50% and another for polyester<30%). If the table only gives a single breaking strength value without separating it by polyester percentage, it doesn’t help me because I can’t tell which blend it applies to. Tables that cover the same yarn and indices but present strength as one combined value should be left out, even if everything else looks similar.

query: High-Temperature Mechanical Property Parameters of Steel

outs.instruction: I’m trying to compare steels based on how their strength holds up as the temperature rises, because I need numbers I can reference directly for elevated-temperature design. The most useful tables for me are the ones that list yield or proof strength values at multiple temperatures, so I can see the trend as temperature increases. I’m fine if the table distinguishes by product form like plate thickness, bolt size, or stud size, as long as the core content is still short-term yield/proof strength versus temperature. What I don’t want are creep or long-duration strength tables that focus on time-dependent values (like 10-hour or 100,000-hour properties), because that’s a different design check. Also, any table that isn’t clearly reporting mechanical property parameters for steel should be excluded since it doesn’t help me make a material comparison.

For this topic I’m specifically working on long-service high-temperature design, so I only want tables that are centered on time-dependent strength at temperature. The tables I need should be about creep-related or long-term allowable strength metrics, like Rp or RD values given for a stated exposure time (for example 10 hours or 100,000 hours) across a temperature range. If a table mixes those long-term values together with regular room-temperature or short-term yield/proof strength data (such as Rp0.2 or ReL at 20–600°C without a service time), it becomes hard for me to isolate what I’m calculating and I don’t want it. Likewise, tables that are just generic or unclear data grids without an obvious steel-property meaning aren’t useful here. In short, keep the results tightly focused on long-duration high-temperature strength only, not general strength-versus-temperature listings.

I’m trying to pick steel grades for a component that will run hot, so I need tables that let me compare how the material’s strength changes as the temperature goes up. The most useful tables are the ones where several columns are all about mechanical strength values at different temperature points, so I can scan across temperatures for each grade. I also want the columns to stay focused on those high-temperature property numbers rather than mixing in lots of geometry or product-dimension information. Tables that devote a major column to sizes like thickness ranges, bolt/stud dimensions, or other fit-up details are not what I’m after, because that turns it into a sizing chart instead of a material-property reference. If the table’s columns are mainly about dimensions or hardware sizing with only secondary strength data, I’d rather exclude it.

I’m collecting reliable material data so I can compare different steel grades for service at elevated temperatures. The most useful tables are the ones where each row is a specific steel grade or designation, and the columns list its mechanical property values measured at multiple temperatures. Please prioritize tables that give high‑temperature yield/0.2% proof strength values (like ReL, Rp0.2, or Rp) across a temperature range, since that lets me compare grades directly. Tables that mainly organize results by product size or thickness are still fine as long as the steel grade remains the thing being described in the row. Exclude tables that are garbled, unlabeled, or don’t clearly identify a steel grade per row, because I can’t trace those values back to a specific material.

I’m trying to compare how different steel grades hold up when the temperature gets high, but I want the data to be presented in a way that reflects long-duration service rather than quick room-temperature-style strength. So I’m looking for tables where each row is a steel grade and the values are strength parameters reported for a stated exposure time at elevated temperatures. It’s especially useful if the table explicitly labels the strength as something like an Rp value tied to a fixed number of hours (for example 10 h or 100,000 h) across multiple temperature columns. Tables that only list the instantaneous yield strength at different temperatures without any time basis are still about high-temperature properties, but they don’t match what I need for sustained operation. Please exclude those time-free yield-at-temperature tables and keep the ones that make the duration part of the reported parameter.
